perturbation.original_text: 13 DISPUTES 13.1 In the event of any dispute or difference arising between the Parties relating to or arising out of this Agreement, including the implementation, execution, interpretation, rectification, termination or cancellation of this Agreement, the chief executive officers of the Parties or any of their designated officials shall upon request by any Party meet to attempt to settle such dispute or difference, and failing settlement within a period of 7 (seven) business days from such a request, the said dispute or difference shall on demand by any Party be submitted to arbitration in Johannesburg in accordance with the Rules of the Arbitration Foundation of Southern Africa by an arbitrator or arbitrato rs appointed by the Foundation.
perturbation.changed_text: 13 DISPUTES 13.1 In the event of any dispute or difference arising between the Parties relating to or arising out of this Agreement, including the implementation, execution, interpretation, rectification, termination or cancellation of this Agreement, the chief executive officers of the Parties or any of their designated officials shall upon request by any Party meet to attempt to settle such dispute or difference, and failing settlement within a period of 7 (seven) business days from such a request, the said dispute or difference shall on demand by any Party be submitted to a method of dispute resolution agreed by both parties.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the specific arbitration clause and replacing it with a vague agreement to find a 'method of dispute resolution agreed by both parties', the contract introduces ambiguity. While it doesn't explicitly contradict other sections, it weakens the enforceability of the dispute resolution process. Section 17.2 states that the parties submit to the jurisdiction of a specific court. If arbitration is no longer guaranteed, the interplay between agreeing on a resolution method and resorting to court becomes uncertain, contradicting the intent of Section 17.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Subject to the provisions of this Agr eement, the Parties hereto hereby consent and submit to the non- exclusive jurisdiction of the North Gauteng Provincial Division of the High Court of the Republic of South Africa in any dispute arising from or in connection with this Agreement. The Parties agree that any costs awarded will be recoverable in accordance with the High Court tariff, determined on an attorney -and-own- client scale.
perturbation.justification: The original text specifies arbitration in Johannesburg as the dispute resolution method. The changed text replaces this with a vague agreement to find a 'method of dispute resolution agreed by both parties'. This creates a contradiction with Section 17.2, which submits to the jurisdiction of the North Gauteng Provincial Division of the High Court. The original text's clear arbitration clause ensured disputes were handled outside the court system initially, whereas the change introduces uncertainty about whether disputes will go to arbitration or directly to court, potentially contradicting the intent and process outlined in Section 17.2. Therefore, the removal of a specific arbitration clause and replacement with a vague agreement contradicts section 17.2.

perturbation.original_text: 16.1 The Parties hereto choose domicilium citandi et executandi for all purposes of and in connection with this Agree ment as follows – 16.1.1 AfriGIS : - JV Communicatio For the attention of: The Managing Director Physical add ress: Rigel Park Block A 446 Rigel Avenue South Erasmusrand Email: legal@afrigis.co.za 16.1.2 Client : For the attention of: The Managing Director Physical address: ………………………….. ………………………….. ………………………… Fax /Email: ………………………….
perturbation.changed_text: 16.1 The Parties hereto choose domicilium citandi et executandi for all purposes of and in connection with this Agree ment as follows – 16.1.1 AfriGIS : - JV Communicatio For the attention of: The Managing Director Physical address: Rigel Park Block A 446 Rigel Avenue South Erasmusrand Email: legal@afrigis.co.za 16.1.2 Client : For the attention of: The Managing Director Physical address: See Annexure A Fax /Email: See Annexure A.
perturbation.explanation: By replacing the Client's address and contact details with 'See Annexure A', and then omitting Annexure A entirely from the contract, a critical contradiction is created. Section 16 relies on this information for delivering legal notices. This now means that the notifications won't be properly sent which will be a breach of contract. While the obligation to provide notices still exists (Section 16.3 and 16.4), there is no valid address, thus making Section 16 practically unenforceable.
perturbation.contradicted_text: All notices, demands, legal proceedings, communications or pay ments intended for any Party shall be made or given at such Party’s domicilium for the time being.
perturbation.justification: The changed text replaces the client's address and contact details with 'See Annexure A', but Annexure A is omitted from the contract. The contradicted text states that notices shall be made at the party's domicilium. Because the changed text removes the address without providing an alternative, it creates a contradiction since the notices cannot be delivered to the client as required in the contradicted text. The explanation correctly states that this makes Section 16 practically unenforceable, confirming a strong in-text contradiction according to the Omissions definition.

perturbation.original_text: 3.3 The Receiving Party agrees that it will, during or after the course of their relationship and/or the term of this Agreement as described in clause 9, keep the Confidential Information in the strictest confidence and will not disclose it to any third party for any reason or purpose whatsoever without the prior written consent of the Disclosing Party, save in accordance with t he provisions of this Agreement, and the Parties undertake to each other that their holding and subsidiary companies or agents shall be bound by the provisions of this Agreement. For avoidance of doubt, in this Agreement “third party” means any party other than Client and AfriGIS and their holding and subsidiary companies or agents.
perturbation.changed_text: 3.3 The Receiving Party agrees that it will, during the term of this Agreement as described in clause 9, keep the Confidential Information confidential and will not disclose it to any third party for any purpose whatsoever without the prior written consent of the Disclosing Party, save in accordance with t he provisions of this Agreement. For avoidance of doubt, in this Agreement “third party” means any party other than Client and AfriGIS and their holding and subsidiary companies or agents.
perturbation.explanation: Removing 'or after the course of their relationship' weakens the protection of Confidential Information after the agreement's term. This creates a contradiction with the implied intent of preserving confidentiality beyond the formal relationship, especially when clause 5 states that the receiving party should be according to data privacy legislation, as amended from time to time.
perturbation.contradicted_text: The Receiving Party undertake s not to use the Confidential Information for any purpose other than – 5.1 that for which it is disclosed; 5.2 in accordance with the provisions of this Agreement; and 5.3 in accordance with applicable data privacy legislation, as amended from time to time.
perturbation.justification: The original text protects confidential information 'during or after the course of their relationship,' while the changed text removes 'or after the course of their relationship,' limiting the protection to only during the agreement's term. This directly contradicts clause 5, which states that the Receiving Party must handle Confidential Information in accordance with data privacy legislation 'as amended from time to time.' This legislation could require maintaining confidentiality beyond the term of the agreement, creating a direct contradiction between the limited term in the changed text and the potentially longer obligation required by clause 5.

perturbation.original_text: 1.2.8 “Client ” means Client Name, a company incorporated in terms of the laws of South Africa which provides ………………..
perturbation.explanation: By removing the definition of what the client provides the NDA becomes contradictory. The clause 2.1 states that: Client is a company which provides ……………… .. This means the clause is now incompletel. Other clauses such as 2.3 mention ‘proprietary discussions pertaining to…’ and that will not be understandable, because you don’t know what is being provided.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Client is a company which provides ……………… .
perturbation.justification: The original text defines the client and its provision. The changed text removes the description of what the client provides. The contradicted text says 'Client is a company which provides...' but now there is no information on what the client provides, because the changed text removed that definition. Thus the changed text contradicts the contradicted text, as it has created an incompleteness where there was originally a full definition. This fits the definition of Omissions - In Text Contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 8 EXCLUDED CONFIDENTIAL INFORMATION The obligations of the Receiving Party pursuant to the provisions of this Agreement shall not apply to any Confidential Information that – 8.1 is or was known to , or in the possession of the Receiving Party prior to disclosure thereof by the Disclosing Party; 8.2 is or becomes publicly known, otherwise than as a result of a breach of this Agreement by the Receiving Party; 8.3 is developed independently of the Disclosing P arty by the Receiving Party in circumstances that do not amount to a breach of the provisions of this Agreement; 8.4 is disclosed by the Receiving Party to satisfy an order of a court of competent jurisdiction or to comply with the provisions of any law or r egulation in force from time to time; provided that in these circumstances, the Receiving Party shall advise the Disclosing Party to take whatever steps it deems necessary to protect its interests in this regard and provided further that the Receiving Part y will disclose only that portion of the information which it is legally required to disclose and the Receiving Party will use its reasonable endeavours to protect the confidentiality of such information to the greatest extent possible in the circumstances ; 8.5 is disclosed to a third party pursuant to the prior written authorisation of the Disclosing Party; and 8.6 is received from a third party in circumstances that do not result in a breach of the provisions of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: 8 EXCLUDED CONFIDENTIAL INFORMATION The obligations of the Receiving Party pursuant to the provisions of this Agreement shall not apply to any Confidential Information that – 8.1 is or was known to , or in the possession of the Receiving Party prior to disclosure thereof by the Disclosing Party; 8.2 is or becomes publicly known, otherwise than as a result of a breach of this Agreement by the Receiving Party; 8.3 is developed independently of the Disclosing P arty by the Receiving Party in circumstances that do not amount to a breach of the provisions of this Agreement;
perturbation.explanation: By removing the exclusions for legally compelled disclosure, authorized third-party disclosures, and information received from third parties, the contract creates a contradiction with general legal principles. Section 8.4 states that legal disclosures can be made if the other party is adviced to take steps in order to protect its interests. This creates a contradiction since the protection of the receiving party is not taken into account, while Section 3.5.2 that the disclosure of the Confidential Information to a third party may cause irreparable loss, harm and damage to the Disclosing Party.
perturbation.contradicted_text: that the unauthorised disclosure of the Confidential Information to a third party may cause irreparable loss, harm and damage to the Disclosing Party. Accordingly, the Receiving Party indemnifies and holds the Disclosing Party harmless against any loss, claim, harm or damage, of whatev er nature, suffered or sustained by the Disclosing Party pursuant to a breach by the Receiving Party of the provisions of this Agreement.
perturbation.justification: The original text in Section 8 outlined specific exceptions to the Receiving Party's confidentiality obligations, including legally compelled disclosures (8.4), authorized third-party disclosures (8.5), and information received from third parties (8.6). The changed text omits these exceptions. This omission creates a direct contradiction with the general principle stated in the Contradicted Text (Section 3.5.2), which states that unauthorized disclosure to a third party may cause irreparable harm and damage to the Disclosing Party. The original Section 8 acknowledged scenarios where disclosure was permissible. By removing these permissible scenarios, the changed Section 8 now implies that ANY disclosure is a breach, thereby directly contradicting the original exemptions and making the Receiving Party liable even in scenarios previously deemed acceptable, creating a strong in-text contradiction according to the Omissions definition.
